sections.Indications for mechanical ventilation: Respiratory failure is classified into failure to maintain adequate ventilation (CNS depression, nerve/neuromuscular failure, respiratory muscle failure, or mechanical load exceeding muscle capacity, e.g. low compliance in ARDS or high resistance in COPD) and failure to maintain adequate oxygenation (V/Q mismatch, shunt, diffusion impairment, or inadequate ventilation). Airway-patency failure is a straightforward indication for intubation, but mechanical ventilation is only needed if other respiratory failure causes co-exist (e.g. GCS<9 in trauma patients). Main indications in respiratory failure: hypercapnic acidosis, dyspnoea with increased work of breathing, or severe hypoxaemia unresponsive to oxygen (PaO2<60 mmHg with FiO2>=50%). Clinical signs of increased work of breathing: nasal flaring, mouth breathing, accessory/expiratory muscle use, tracheal tug, intercostal recession, paradoxical abdominal movement, tachypnoea, tachycardia, hyper-/hypotension, diaphoresis. Note: initiating mechanical ventilation during shock reduced the proportion of cardiac output directed to the respiratory muscles ~7-fold in an experimental setting.
sections.Aims of mechanical ventilation: The goal is to support gas exchange and sustain life until the cause of respiratory failure resolves, guided by 'first do no harm'. Aims: CO2 elimination, improved oxygenation, and relief of respiratory distress by unloading the respiratory muscles. Ventilation can itself cause harm - ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI) from over-stretching/cyclical reopening of alveoli, and ventilator-induced diaphragmatic dysfunction (VIDD) from prolonged unloading. Ventilator settings also affect intrathoracic and pulmonary vascular pressures, impacting heart function.
sections.PCO2 and pH targets: Normal PCO2/pH are typically targeted in healthy lungs (perioperatively) and in any brain injury, to avoid raising intracranial pressure - but a normal PCO2 is not mandatory at any cost in all patients. Permissive hypercapnia targets a higher-than-normal PaCO2 by using a low tidal volume/minute ventilation, to prevent lung over-distension, which can cause VILI (low FRC, e.g. ARDS) or dynamic hyperinflation (flow-limited patients, e.g. COPD/asthma). Patients ventilated this way often need deep sedation and/or paralysis to tolerate passive ventilation.
sections.Oxygenation targets: An SpO2 above 90-92% is a reasonable target in most patients. Because the oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve plateaus, a supraphysiological PaO2 gives no significant additional oxygen delivery benefit; targeting hyperoxia has shown no benefit and may be associated with worse outcomes. Oxygen delivery (DO2) = cardiac output x (Hb x SaO2 x 1.34 + dissolved oxygen [PaO2 x 0.003]) - the haemoglobin-bound term dominates, so once SaO2 is near-maximal, raising PaO2 further contributes very little extra oxygen delivery compared to correcting anaemia or cardiac output.
sections.Means to deliver positive pressure venti: Two interfaces exist: the endotracheal/tracheostomy tube (invasive) or a tight-fitting facial mask (non-invasive, NIMV). Endotracheal tube: protects against aspiration, relieves fixed upper airway obstruction, permits secretion suction, gives a stable leak-proof connection - but needs sedation, prevents effective cough and speech, and removes upper-airway infection-protective reflexes. NIV mask: comfortable, preserves cough/speech, needs no sedation - but requires patient cooperation, gives no aspiration protection, no suctioning, and no guaranteed leak-proof connection. Variable airway obstruction (e.g. OSA) can be managed with NIMV; fixed obstruction cannot.
sections.NIMV: NIMV avoids intubation and device-related complications (VAP, catheter-related infections). Contraindications: cardiac/respiratory arrest, inability to protect the airway, inability to manage secretions, facial/upper-airway trauma/surgery/obstruction, intractable emesis or GI bleeding, and shock (a relative contraindication and a common cause of NIMV failure). Close monitoring is required for the first 1-2 hours to catch early failure. Two main modes: CPAP (mainly hypoxaemic failure, e.g. cardiogenic pulmonary oedema) and BIPAP/IPAP+EPAP (preferred for hypercapnic failure, e.g. COPD). Initiation criteria include: absence of contraindications, moderate-severe dyspnoea, tachypnoea (>24/min obstructive, >30/min restrictive), signs of increased work of breathing, acute-on-chronic COPD failure with PaCO2>45mmHg, pH<7.35, hypoxaemia uncorrected by oxygen alone (use with caution), PaO2/FiO2<300. Termination criteria: haemodynamic instability, decreasing consciousness, worsening pH/PaCO2/PaO2, tachypnoea>30, intense dyspnoea, inability to clear secretions, agitation/intolerance with progressive failure. Evidence: NIV in AECOPD reduced mortality 46% and intubation need 65%; in cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, reduced mortality 35% and intubation need 50%; in hypoxaemic ARDS/pneumonia (non-COVID), evidence is inconsistent with high failure rates and possible increased mortality; in COVID-19 ARDS, both CPAP and high-flow nasal oxygen reduced need for invasive ventilation. Failure risk factors: disease severity, comorbidities, shock, impaired consciousness, non-COVID ARDS, severe acidosis, high tidal volume/minute ventilation, failure to improve within 1-2h.

questions.question: A 68-year-old man is brought in after a large opioid overdose. He is difficult to rouse with a respiratory rate of 8/min. Arterial blood gas shows PaCO2 8.5 kPa (64 mmHg), PaO2 11 kPa (83 mmHg) on room air, and a normal alveolar-arterial oxygen gradient. Which mechanism best accounts for his respiratory failure?
options: A: Ventilation-perfusion mismatch
B: Central depression of respiratory drive
C: Intrapulmonary shunt
D: Impaired alveolar-capillary diffusion
E: Respiratory muscle fatigue from an increased mechanical load
questions.explanation: Opioids depress the central respiratory drive, causing failure of adequate ventilation with a normal A-a gradient - the hallmark being hypercapnia without a widened gradient. V/Q mismatch, shunt, and diffusion impairment all widen the A-a gradient, and muscle fatigue would be unusual as a primary event in an otherwise well patient with no increased load.
questions.topic: classification respiratory failure ventilatory oxygenation CNS depression opioid A-a gradient

questions.question: A 24-year-old man with a severe traumatic brain injury has a GCS of 6. He is breathing spontaneously with normal oxygen saturations and unremarkable arterial blood gases. What is the most appropriate management of his airway and ventilation?
options: A: Continue close observation without airway intervention while gas exchange remains normal
B: Intubate for airway protection, and provide mechanical ventilation
C: Intubate for airway protection only, leaving him to breathe spontaneously through the tube
D: Apply non-invasive ventilation to secure ventilation without sedation
E: Provide high-flow nasal oxygen and reassess the GCS hourly
questions.explanation: A GCS below 9 in a trauma patient is an indication for intubation to protect the airway; once intubated, mechanical ventilation is provided, since a deeply obtunded patient will not reliably ventilate spontaneously through a cuffed tube. Intubating for airway protection only, without providing ventilation, overlooks this need, and continued observation, non-invasive ventilation, or high-flow oxygen all leave an unprotected airway in a patient who cannot guard it himself.
questions.topic: GCS trauma intubation indication airway protection ventilation obtunded

questions.question: A 30-year-old woman with an asthma exacerbation on high-flow oxygen develops nasal flaring, tracheal tug, and paradoxical abdominal movement. Her respiratory rate rises from 22 to 34/min despite repeated bronchodilators. How are these findings best interpreted?
options: A: Effective recruitment of accessory muscles that can be allowed to continue without escalation
B: Signs of increased work of breathing and impending respiratory muscle fatigue
C: A predominantly psychogenic hyperventilation pattern given the rapid rate
D: Early features of a tension pneumothorax rather than respiratory muscle overload
E: Tremor and tachypnoea attributable to beta-agonist therapy rather than distress
questions.explanation: Nasal flaring, tracheal tug and (particularly) paradoxical abdominal movement indicate a failing respiratory pump with impending muscle fatigue; their appearance alongside a rising respiratory rate despite treatment signals the need to reassess for ventilatory support. Framing this accessory-muscle use as effective, benign compensation that can continue without escalation misreads a sign of distress as reassuring, and the remaining options attribute these objective failure signs to less threatening causes such as psychogenic hyperventilation, pneumothorax, or a drug effect.
questions.topic: signs increased work of breathing tracheal tug paradoxical abdominal fatigue asthma escalation

questions.question: A patient in septic shock on high-dose noradrenaline develops severe respiratory distress with markedly increased work of breathing. Beyond improving gas exchange, what additional effect can initiating mechanical ventilation have?
options: A: It redirects a substantial share of cardiac output away from the overworked respiratory muscles toward other organs
B: It raises cardiac output by improving venous return, which is the principal reason it helps in septic shock
C: It lowers systemic vascular resistance, thereby reducing vasopressor requirements
D: It increases oxygen delivery chiefly by raising the dissolved oxygen content of the blood
E: It reduces myocardial oxygen demand mainly by directly slowing the heart rate
questions.explanation: Hard-working respiratory muscles can consume a disproportionate fraction of cardiac output in shock; taking over ventilation reduces this demand and can free perfusion for other organs. Positive-pressure ventilation tends to reduce rather than augment venous return, so it does not raise cardiac output by this route; it does not primarily act by lowering systemic vascular resistance either, and the dissolved-oxygen contribution to oxygen delivery is negligible, so raising it is not the mechanism at play here.
questions.topic: mechanical ventilation shock cardiac output respiratory muscle demand redistribution

questions.question: A patient with moderate ARDS is ventilated at 6 mL/kg predicted body weight. Plateau pressure is 29 cmH2O, arterial pH 7.28, and PaCO2 7.3 kPa (55 mmHg). Oxygenation is adequate and there is no intracranial pathology. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: A: Accept the current hypercapnia and acidosis while maintaining the lung-protective tidal volume
B: Increase the tidal volume to bring the PaCO2 back toward normal
C: Give intravenous sodium bicarbonate to correct the acidosis
D: Raise the respiratory rate substantially to normalise pH
E: Add a further increase in PEEP to improve CO2 clearance
questions.explanation: Permissive hypercapnia accepts a raised PaCO2 and mild acidosis to avoid the higher tidal volumes and plateau pressures that worsen lung injury; with adequate oxygenation and no raised intracranial pressure, no change is needed. Raising the tidal volume abandons lung protection, giving intravenous bicarbonate is not indicated for this compensated respiratory acidosis, substantially raising the respiratory rate risks gas trapping, and adding further PEEP is not a means of CO2 removal here.
questions.topic: permissive hypercapnia ARDS lung protective tidal volume acidosis acceptable

questions.question: A patient with ARDS is ventilated with a low tidal volume, accepting the resulting hypercapnia. All of the following are recognised rationales for this approach EXCEPT:
options: A: Limiting alveolar over-distension in a lung with a reduced functional residual capacity
B: Reducing the risk of ventilator-induced lung injury from high distending pressures
C: Restoring a normal PaCO2, which should take priority over limiting tidal volume in ARDS
D: Accepting a trade-off between ventilation targets and lung protection
E: Avoiding the higher plateau pressures that larger tidal volumes would generate
questions.explanation: Permissive hypercapnia is a deliberate departure from normalising PaCO2 when doing so would require injurious tidal volumes; prioritising a normal PaCO2 over lung protection is the opposite of its rationale, making that the EXCEPT answer here. Limiting alveolar over-distension in a lung with reduced functional residual capacity, reducing the risk of ventilator-induced lung injury from high distending pressures, accepting a trade-off between ventilation targets and lung protection, and avoiding the higher plateau pressures that larger tidal volumes would generate, all correctly describe why the strategy is used.
questions.topic: permissive hypercapnia rationale negative stem normalise PaCO2 lung protection EXCEPT

questions.question: A ventilated patient has an SpO2 of 98% on FiO2 0.6, with no hypoxaemia and no carbon monoxide exposure. What is the most appropriate next step for the FiO2?
options: A: Reduce the FiO2, targeting an SpO2 around 90-92%
B: Keep the FiO2 at 0.6 to preserve a safety margin against desaturation
C: Increase the FiO2 toward 1.0 to maximise arterial oxygen content
D: Reduce the FiO2 only once the SpO2 falls below 88%
E: Leave the FiO2 unchanged but add PEEP to permit a later reduction
questions.explanation: With adequate oxygenation, FiO2 should be weaned toward the lowest level maintaining an SpO2 of roughly 90-92%, since targeting supraphysiological PaO2 confers no benefit and hyperoxia has been linked to worse outcomes. Keeping the FiO2 at its current level or raising it toward 1.0 both expose the patient to hyperoxia without benefit; waiting to reduce FiO2 only once SpO2 falls below 88% tolerates unnecessary hyperoxia until desaturation, and leaving FiO2 unchanged while adding PEEP defers the straightforward step of weaning FiO2.
questions.topic: oxygenation target SpO2 90-92 hyperoxia harm FiO2 weaning

questions.question: A ventilated patient has haemoglobin 68 g/L, SaO2 98%, cardiac output 5.5 L/min, and PaO2 12 kPa (90 mmHg). Which single intervention would increase oxygen delivery the most?
options: A: Transfusing red cells to correct the anaemia
B: Increasing the FiO2 to raise the PaO2 further
C: Increasing PEEP to recruit additional lung units
D: Starting an inodilator to modestly raise the cardiac output
E: Adding inhaled nitric oxide to improve ventilation-perfusion matching
questions.explanation: Oxygen delivery is dominated by the haemoglobin-bound term (Hb x SaO2 x 1.34); with SaO2 already near-maximal and a haemoglobin of 68 g/L, correcting the anaemia gives by far the largest gain. Raising the FiO2 to increase PaO2 further adds only to the negligible dissolved-oxygen term, and while cardiac output is a genuine determinant of delivery, the modest achievable rise from an inodilator is outweighed by correcting the marked anaemia here.
questions.topic: oxygen delivery DO2 haemoglobin cardiac output dissolved oxygen transfusion anaemia

questions.question: Based on the ventilatory response curves shown, how does a hypoxaemic patient's increase in minute ventilation for a given rise in PaCO2 compare with that of a normoxic patient?
options: A: Greater, because hypoxaemia shifts the response leftward and steepens its slope
B: Similar, because PaCO2 is the dominant stimulus and hypoxaemia has little effect on the slope
C: Smaller, because hypoxaemia blunts the central chemoreceptor response to carbon dioxide
D: Greater at first, then lower, as hypoxaemia causes ventilation to decline at higher PaCO2
E: Unchanged in slope but shifted only to a higher resting minute ventilation
questions.explanation: The curves show that hypoxaemia (like acidosis) shifts the ventilation-PaCO2 relationship leftward and steepens it, so a hypoxaemic patient increases minute ventilation more for the same rise in PaCO2 - relevant to why hypoxaemic patients often show marked respiratory drive and distress. Describing the response as smaller, as if hypoxaemia blunted the central chemoreceptor response to carbon dioxide, describes the opposite direction, and the remaining options misread the curve's position or slope.
questions.topic: ventilatory response curve hypoxia acidosis left shift respiratory drive PaCO2 slope

questions.question: A patient with pneumonia has copious purulent secretions, a reduced conscious level, and an impaired cough, and now needs positive pressure ventilation. Which interface is most appropriate?
options: A: Endotracheal intubation, allowing airway protection and direct suctioning
B: Non-invasive ventilation via a well-fitted oronasal mask
C: Non-invasive ventilation via a helmet interface to improve tolerance
D: CPAP by nasal mask combined with regular chest physiotherapy
E: High-flow nasal oxygen with close observation
questions.explanation: A reduced conscious level with copious secretions and an impaired cough favours an endotracheal tube, which permits airway protection and direct suctioning - capabilities the non-invasive options lack. Effective non-invasive ventilation (B-E) depends on an adequate cough and conscious level to be safe, both of which are compromised here.
questions.section: Means to deliver positive pressure ventilation
questions.topic: interface selection invasive NIV secretions consciousness cough airway protection

questions.question: An alert, haemodynamically stable patient has acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema with an SpO2 of 84% on high-flow oxygen and a normal PaCO2. Which initial non-invasive mode is most appropriate?
options: A: CPAP
B: BIPAP with a high level of inspiratory pressure support
C: Bilevel ventilation titrated to bring down the PaCO2
D: Invasive volume-controlled ventilation
E: Low-flow nasal cannula oxygen alone
questions.explanation: CPAP is the preferred initial non-invasive mode for hypoxaemic failure from cardiogenic pulmonary oedema; bilevel modes are favoured when hypercapnic failure predominates, which is not the case here given the normal PaCO2. Reaching for a bilevel mode, whether with high inspiratory pressure support or titrated to lower PaCO2, is therefore not the first choice; escalating straight to invasive ventilation is unwarranted in a stable, alert patient, and low-flow nasal cannula oxygen is inadequate for the degree of hypoxaemia.
questions.topic: CPAP BIPAP selection cardiogenic pulmonary oedema hypoxaemic hypercapnic

questions.question: A patient with moderate ARDS from pneumonia (not COVID-19) is being considered for non-invasive ventilation as an alternative to intubation. Which statement best reflects current evidence?
options: A: NIV carries a high failure rate here and may raise mortality, so it should be used cautiously with close monitoring
B: NIV reliably reduces mortality in hypoxaemic ARDS and is a sound first-line alternative to intubation
C: NIV should replace invasive ventilation once ARDS is diagnosed, given its lower complication profile
D: NIV is inappropriate in ARDS because its main effect is to delay necessary intubation
E: NIV works about as well in pneumonia-related ARDS as it does in COPD exacerbations
questions.explanation: In de novo hypoxaemic failure and non-COVID ARDS, NIV has high failure rates and some studies show increased mortality, so it is used cautiously with close monitoring rather than routinely. B and E over-generalise from the strong COPD/oedema evidence to a setting where NIV performs much less well; C overstates its role, and D hardens a genuine concern (delayed intubation) into a blanket exclusion.
questions.topic: NIV hypoxaemic ARDS evidence failure rate mortality caution non-COVID

questions.question: A haemodynamically stable, cooperative patient with COVID-19-related ARDS is hypoxaemic. Which non-invasive strategy is best supported for reducing the need for invasive ventilation?
options: A: Either CPAP or high-flow nasal oxygen
B: BIPAP, whereas CPAP and high-flow oxygen have not been shown to help here
C: Early intubation in preference to any non-invasive support
D: High-flow nasal oxygen, but only after a trial of CPAP has failed
E: Awake prone positioning without any oxygen-delivery device
questions.explanation: In COVID-19-related ARDS, both CPAP and high-flow nasal oxygen have been shown to reduce the need for invasive ventilation, distinguishing this population from ARDS of other causes. B misattributes the benefit to BIPAP alone, C forgoes a supported non-invasive trial, D imposes an unnecessary sequence, and E omits the oxygen-delivery component central to the evidence.
questions.topic: COVID-19 ARDS CPAP high-flow nasal oxygen invasive ventilation reduction evidence

questions.question: A patient is started on BIPAP for an acute exacerbation of COPD in the emergency department. What is the most important element of care in the first 1-2 hours?
options: A: Close monitoring to detect early NIV failure and allow timely escalation
B: Transfer to a general ward once the mask is fitted and the initial gas improves
C: Adding sedation to improve mask tolerance regardless of the respiratory trend
D: Fixing the settings and rechecking an arterial gas the following morning
E: Switching to CPAP after the first hour to reduce the work of breathing
questions.explanation: The first 1-2 hours are when early failure declares itself, so close monitoring of gases, consciousness and haemodynamics allows timely escalation; failure to improve within this window is itself a predictor of NIV failure. Each of the other options delays recognition of a deteriorating patient or introduces sedation into a non-intubated patient who may be failing.
questions.topic: NIV monitoring first hours early failure recognition escalation COPD

questions.question: Two hours into a trial of NIV for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure, a patient becomes agitated with a rising PaCO2 and a falling pH despite optimised settings and mask fit. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: A: Proceed to endotracheal intubation and invasive ventilation
B: Deepen sedation to improve tolerance and continue NIV unchanged
C: Switch from BIPAP to CPAP and reassess in a further two hours
D: Increase the inspiratory pressure and extend the NIV trial
E: Add high-flow nasal oxygen alongside the continued NIV
questions.explanation: Worsening pH and PaCO2 with agitation and intolerance despite optimisation are termination criteria indicating NIV failure, so escalation to invasive ventilation is appropriate. Deepening sedation to improve tolerance is hazardous in a failing, non-intubated patient, and switching to CPAP, increasing inspiratory pressure, or adding high-flow nasal oxygen alongside the continued NIV all prolong an ineffective trial while the patient deteriorates.
questions.topic: NIV failure termination criteria escalation invasive ventilation agitation

questions.question: All of the following are recognised clinical signs of increased work of breathing EXCEPT:
options: A: Nasal flaring
B: Intercostal recession
C: A falling heart rate
D: Tracheal tug
E: Diaphoresis
questions.explanation: Increased work of breathing is typically accompanied by tachycardia, not a falling heart rate; bradycardia is not among the recognised signs and, if anything, may herald a more ominous process such as exhaustion or hypoxaemic decompensation. The remaining options are all listed features of increased work of breathing.
questions.topic: signs increased work of breathing negative stem bradycardia heart rate EXCEPT

questions.question: Regarding contraindications to non-invasive ventilation (NIV):

statements.label: A
statements.text: Facial or upper-airway trauma with potential obstruction is a contraindication.

statements.label: B
statements.text: An inability to protect the airway is a contraindication.

statements.label: C
statements.text: An inability to clear secretions is a contraindication.

statements.label: D
statements.text: An acute exacerbation of COPD is a contraindication.

questions.explanation: Facial or upper-airway trauma with potential obstruction, an inability to protect the airway, and an inability to clear secretions are all recognised contraindications to NIV. The claim that an acute exacerbation of COPD is a contraindication is false — an acute COPD exacerbation is in fact one of the strongest indications for NIV, not a contraindication; the intuition that a severely unwell COPD patient is 'too sick' for NIV is a common misconception.
questions.topic: NIV contraindications COPD indication trap airway secretions facial trauma

questions.question: Regarding criteria used to decide on initiating NIV:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A respiratory rate above 30/min supports initiation in a patient with restrictive lung disease.

statements.label: B
statements.text: A PaCO2 below 45 mmHg supports initiation in an acute-on-chronic COPD exacerbation.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A PaO2/FiO2 ratio below 300 supports initiation.

statements.label: D
statements.text: The respiratory-rate threshold for initiation is the same in obstructive and restrictive disease.

questions.explanation: A respiratory rate above 30/min supporting initiation in restrictive lung disease, and a PaO2/FiO2 ratio below 300 supporting initiation, both match the recognised initiation criteria. The claim that a PaCO2 below 45 mmHg supports initiation in an acute-on-chronic COPD exacerbation is false — it is a PaCO2 above 45 mmHg (with pH below 7.35) that supports initiation in acute-on-chronic COPD; a low PaCO2 does not. The claim that the respiratory-rate threshold for initiation is the same in obstructive and restrictive disease is also false — the thresholds differ (above 24/min in obstructive versus above 30/min in restrictive disease).
questions.topic: NIV initiation criteria respiratory rate PaCO2 PaO2 FiO2 threshold obstructive restrictive

questions.question: Regarding criteria for terminating a trial of NIV:

statements.label: A
statements.text: An improving PaO2 during NIV is an indication to terminate the trial.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Haemodynamic instability is an indication to terminate the trial.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A decreasing level of consciousness is an indication to terminate the trial.

statements.label: D
statements.text: An inability to clear secretions is an indication to terminate the trial.

questions.explanation: Haemodynamic instability, a decreasing level of consciousness, and an inability to clear secretions are all recognised indications to terminate an NIV trial. The claim that an improving PaO2 during NIV is an indication to terminate the trial is false and points the wrong way — it is a worsening, not improving, PaO2 that indicates termination and escalation; an improving PaO2 is instead a sign the trial is succeeding.
questions.topic: NIV termination criteria consciousness PaO2 direction haemodynamic secretions

questions.question: Comparing an endotracheal tube with a non-invasive ventilation mask as interfaces:

statements.label: A
statements.text: An endotracheal tube provides more reliable protection against aspiration than a mask.

statements.label: B
statements.text: A mask better preserves the ability to cough and speak than an endotracheal tube.

statements.label: C
statements.text: An endotracheal tube generally requires less sedation than a mask.

statements.label: D
statements.text: A mask allows more effective suctioning of lower-airway secretions than a tube.

questions.explanation: An endotracheal tube providing more reliable protection against aspiration, and a mask better preserving the ability to cough and speak, correctly reflect the relative advantages of each interface. The claim that an endotracheal tube generally requires less sedation than a mask is false — an endotracheal tube generally requires more sedation, not less. The claim that a mask allows more effective suctioning of lower-airway secretions than a tube is also false — it is the endotracheal tube, not the mask, that permits direct suctioning of secretions.
questions.section: Means to deliver positive pressure ventilation
questions.topic: endotracheal tube NIV mask comparison sedation suction cough aspiration

questions.question: Regarding unloading the respiratory muscles during mechanical ventilation:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Unloading the respiratory muscles can help prevent respiratory muscle fatigue.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Reducing muscle workload can lessen self-inflicted lung injury from vigorous spontaneous effort.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Prolonged, complete respiratory muscle rest carries no risk to muscle structure or function.

statements.label: D
statements.text: In shock, reducing the respiratory muscles' share of cardiac output is of little clinical importance.

questions.explanation: Unloading the respiratory muscles helping prevent respiratory muscle fatigue, and reducing muscle workload lessening self-inflicted lung injury from vigorous spontaneous effort, are recognised benefits of unloading. The claim that prolonged, complete respiratory muscle rest carries no risk to muscle structure or function is false — prolonged complete rest promotes ventilator-induced diaphragmatic dysfunction and weakness. The claim that reducing the respiratory muscles' share of cardiac output in shock is of little clinical importance is also false — in shock, the respiratory muscles can consume a large share of cardiac output, so reducing that demand is clinically important, not negligible.
questions.topic: unloading respiratory muscles VIDD fatigue self-inflicted lung injury shock cardiac output
